title: A superb solo RPG of paranormal investigation - The Unseen World (review and gameplay)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-FJcDEQ2CB0

Daniel (@dungeondive) fell for this one so hard he printed his own zine rather than wait for the physical copy to ship. The Unseen World, by Thomas P. King, is a solitary gamebook of paranormal investigation in modern Japan — pitched squarely at fans of Fatal Frame, Silent Hill, Ring, and Kairo — where you play a ghost hunter travelling town to town, researching tragic histories, and braving haunted places to capture evidence of the spirit world. The systems are clever: a playing-card challenge deck doubles as a day timer, evidence comes in visual, audio, and data flavours that each demand the right gear, and gathered evidence converts into blog posts, magazine articles, and book chapters that fund the next investigation. Daniel loves that structure — research a haunt to three pieces of information, then investigate, then publish. The extended playthrough is wonderfully atmospheric: rookie investigator Ryusei arrives in an industry town, coaxes rumours from a curious pawn-shop owner and a shrine steward, and pieces together the story of a school abandoned after a chemical fire the town would rather forget — then walks its cold hallways as a distorted chime crackles from dead loudspeakers. Each campaign escalates until a final confrontation in a castle. Highly recommended alongside Little Town and Alone Against Fear.

----

The Unseen World turns ghost-hunting into a research loop — informants, archives, then the haunting itself. Does that slow-burn investigative structure appeal to you more than jump-scare horror gaming, or less?
